title: 2 Corinthians 3
subtitle: The Glory of the New Covenant

heb.paragraph: The noun diakonia (ministry, service) is a key term in 2 Corinthians, appearing eighteen times (more than any other Pauline letter). Paul uses it to describe his apostolic commission as a ‘ministry of the new covenant’ (v. 6). The term’s semantic range includes service, administration, and the content of the message served. Paul’s ministry is not self-appointed but God-given: ‘our competence comes from God’ (v. 5).

The antithesis gramma/pneuma (letter/Spirit) in v. 6 is one of Paul’s most consequential theological distinctions. The ‘letter’ refers to the Mosaic law as an external written code; the ‘Spirit’ refers to the Holy Spirit as the internalising agent of the new covenant. Paul’s point is not that the OT law was bad but that it was incomplete: it could diagnose sin (‘the letter kills’) but could not empower obedience (‘the Spirit gives life’). This echoes Jeremiah’s prophecy of a new covenant written on hearts (Jer 31:33) and Ezekiel’s promise of a new spirit (Ezek 36:26–27).

panels.ctx: The super-apostles apparently carried ‘letters of recommendation’ (v. 1) to bolster their credentials. Paul’s response is audacious: the Corinthian community itself is his letter of recommendation, ‘written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts’ (v. 3). This bold allusion to Exodus 31:18 and Ezekiel 36:26 transforms an ecclesial observation into a covenant-theological claim: the new covenant promised by Jeremiah and Ezekiel has arrived, and its evidence is the Spirit-transformed lives of the Corinthian believers.

cross.note: Jeremiah’s new covenant prophecy—‘I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts’—is the OT foundation for Paul’s letter/Spirit contrast. The new covenant internalises what the old covenant externalised.
Ezekiel’s promise of a ‘new heart’ and ‘new spirit’ complements Jeremiah’s prophecy and supplies the ‘tablets of human hearts’ imagery Paul employs in v. 3.

notes.note: Paul’s opponents carried letters of commendation; Paul has something better: the Corinthian believers themselves. Their transformed lives are a ‘letter from Christ’ (v. 3)—authored by Christ, delivered by Paul, written by the Spirit on human hearts. The contrast with stone tablets directly invokes the Sinai revelation and its replacement by the new covenant.

Paul’s competence is entirely derived: ‘our competence comes from God’ (v. 5). God has made him a minister of the ‘new covenant’—the first explicit use of this phrase in Paul’s extant letters. The letter/Spirit antithesis (v. 6) does not denigrate the OT law but identifies its limitation: it can expose sin but cannot empower righteousness.

Calvin reads Paul’s letter-of-recommendation argument as a powerful ecclesiological statement: the best evidence for a minister’s authenticity is the spiritual fruit of his ministry. Calvin’s treatment of the letter/Spirit contrast is careful: the ‘letter’ that ‘kills’ is not the OT per se but the law considered apart from the Spirit’s empowering work. Calvin insists that the same Spirit who animates the new covenant was also present under the old, but in a preliminary and external mode.

The NET note explains the allusion to Exod 31:18 (stone tablets) and Ezek 36:26 (heart of flesh). Paul conflates these texts to argue that the Corinthians are the living proof of the new covenant’s inauguration.

The NET note discusses ‘the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life’: this is not a hermeneutical principle (literal vs. spiritual interpretation) but a covenant-historical claim about the respective functions of the old and new covenants.

Fee argues that the new-covenant theology of this passage is the theological centre of 2 Corinthians. The Spirit’s work of heart-transformation fulfils the prophetic promises of Jeremiah and Ezekiel, and the Corinthians’ own experience of the Spirit is the proof. Fee emphasises that Paul’s competence-from-God claim (v. 5) is a direct counter to the super-apostles’ self-commendation.

Harris provides detailed analysis of the ‘letter of recommendation’ (systatikas epistolas) motif, noting that letters of commendation were standard in the ancient Mediterranean world. Paul’s subversion of the convention is brilliant: rather than carrying paper credentials, his credential is a living community. Harris traces the OT allusions (Exod 24:12; 31:18; 34:1; Jer 31:33; Ezek 11:19; 36:26) with meticulous care, arguing that Paul weaves them into a composite typology of covenant transition.

sections.header: Verses 7–11 — "The Surpassing Glory of the New Covenant"

heb.paragraph: The noun doxa (glory) appears ten times in vv. 7–11, creating the chapter’s most concentrated theological cluster. Paul constructs a series of qal wahomer (lesser-to-greater) arguments: if the ministry of death came with glory, how much more the ministry of the Spirit? If the ministry of condemnation was glorious, how much more the ministry of righteousness? If the transitory was glorious, how much more the permanent? The argument does not deny the Sinai covenant’s glory but declares it eclipsed by a surpassing glory.

panels.ctx: Paul’s argument draws on the Exodus 34 narrative of Moses’ radiant face after encountering God on Sinai. The glory of the old covenant was real but transitory—it was ‘passing away’ (katargoumenon, vv. 7, 11, 13). The new covenant’s glory, by contrast, is permanent and surpassing. Three comparative pairs structure the argument: death/Spirit (vv. 7–8), condemnation/righteousness (v. 9), transitory/permanent (v. 11). Each comparison concedes glory to the old covenant while affirming the incomparably greater glory of the new.

cross.note: The narrative of Moses’ shining face after receiving the law on Sinai provides the scriptural basis for Paul’s entire argument. Paul’s interpretation (vv. 13–16) will add a layer of typological significance.
The author of Hebrews develops a parallel argument for the superiority of the new covenant, citing Jer 31:31–34 at length. Both authors use covenant comparison as a rhetorical strategy.

notes.note: Paul’s argument is a fortiori (from lesser to greater): if the ministry that brought death (the Mosaic law inscribed on stone, which condemns sinners) came with such glory that the Israelites could not gaze at Moses’ face, how much more glorious is the ministry of the Spirit, which brings righteousness? The argument does not denigrate the old covenant but transcends it.

The old covenant’s glory, though real, has been eclipsed: ‘what was glorious has no glory now in comparison with the surpassing glory’ (v. 10). Paul uses the analogy of starlight at dawn: the stars do not cease to exist, but their light is swallowed by the sun. The permanence of the new covenant (v. 11) is the decisive advantage: the old was always designed to be temporary.

Calvin reads the glory-comparison as a vindication of the continuity between the two covenants: the new does not abolish but fulfils the old. The old covenant’s glory was real, reflecting God’s genuine presence on Sinai. But it was preparatory and transitory, pointing forward to the permanent and surpassing glory of the new covenant in Christ.

The NET note explains that Paul uses the a fortiori argument three times in vv. 7–11, each building on the previous: death→Spirit, condemnation→righteousness, transitory→permanent. The logical structure is cumulative and climactic.

Fee reads the glory-comparison as Paul’s response to opponents who may have emphasised Moses and the Sinai covenant. Fee notes that Paul does not disparage the old covenant but relativises it: its glory was real but has been eclipsed by something incomparably greater. The ministry of the Spirit is the fulfilment of what the Sinai covenant anticipated but could not achieve.

Harris provides the most detailed structural analysis of the three a fortiori arguments, noting that each comparison operates on a different axis: agency (death vs. Spirit), function (condemnation vs. righteousness), and duration (transitory vs. permanent). Harris argues that the verb katargeo (‘to render inoperative,’ vv. 7, 11, 13, 14) is a key term in Paul’s covenant theology: the old covenant has been ‘set aside’ not by annihilation but by fulfilment—as a chrysalis is ‘set aside’ when the butterfly emerges.

heb.paragraph: The noun kalymma (veil) appears four times in vv. 13–16 and controls the passage’s typological argument. In Exodus 34:33–35, Moses veiled his face after speaking with God. Paul’s innovative interpretation identifies the veil’s purpose not as protection from glory’s brightness but as concealment of the glory’s fading. The veil thus symbolises the temporary and concealed nature of the old covenant’s glory. When anyone ‘turns to the Lord,’ the veil is removed (v. 16)—an allusion to Moses removing his veil when entering the Lord’s presence (Exod 34:34).

The verb metamorphoō (to transform) in v. 18 describes the progressive transformation of believers ‘from one degree of glory to another’ (apo doxēs eis doxan). The same verb appears in Rom 12:2 (‘be transformed by the renewing of your mind’) and in the Transfiguration narratives (Matt 17:2; Mark 9:2). The transformation is ongoing (present tense: metamorphoumetha) and Spirit-empowered (‘from the Lord, who is the Spirit’). Unlike Moses’ fading glory, the believer’s transformation increases.

panels.ctx: Paul’s typological interpretation of the Mosaic veil is one of the boldest hermeneutical moves in the NT. He argues that Moses veiled his face not to shield the Israelites from unbearable brightness but to prevent them from seeing that the glory was fading (v. 13). The veil thus becomes a symbol of the old covenant’s concealment of its own obsolescence. Paul then extends the metaphor: the same veil remains ‘when the old covenant is read’ (v. 14)—unbelieving Jews cannot perceive that the old covenant has been fulfilled in Christ. The removal of the veil occurs ‘whenever anyone turns to the Lord’ (v. 16). The climactic verse (v. 18) shifts from Moses to all believers: with unveiled faces, they contemplate the Lord’s glory and are progressively transformed into his image.

cross.note: The Exodus narrative of Moses’ veil is Paul’s primary text. Paul’s interpretation innovates by identifying the veil’s purpose as concealing the fading of glory rather than shielding from glory’s intensity.
The call to be ‘transformed by the renewing of your mind’ uses the same verb (metamorphoō) as 2 Cor 3:18, linking ethical transformation to the glory-beholding process described here.
Paul’s mirror-and-face-to-face imagery in 1 Cor 13 anticipates the unveiled-face imagery of 2 Cor 3:18: both passages describe the progressive clarification of the believer’s perception of God.

notes.note: Paul’s boldness (parrhēsia, v. 12) contrasts with Moses’ veiling. The new-covenant minister speaks openly because the glory he serves is permanent, not fading. Moses veiled his face to prevent the Israelites from seeing the glory’s end—Paul’s innovative reading of Exodus 34.

The veil metaphor extends to Jewish reading of the Torah: ‘to this day the same veil remains when the old covenant is read’ (v. 14). The veil is removed only ‘in Christ’ (v. 14) or ‘whenever anyone turns to the Lord’ (v. 16). Paul identifies ‘the Lord’ of Exodus 34:34 (to whom Moses turned in the tent) with Christ.

The enigmatic statement ‘the Lord is the Spirit’ (v. 17) identifies Christ as the one who removes the veil and grants freedom. Verse 18 is the chapter’s climax: all believers, with unveiled faces, contemplate the Lord’s glory and are being transformed ‘from glory to glory’ by the Spirit. This ongoing transformation is the heart of Paul’s new-covenant theology.

Calvin reads the veil typology as evidence that the old covenant, while genuinely glorious, was designed to point beyond itself to Christ. The veil’s removal in Christ (v. 14) means that the OT can now be read with christological clarity. Verse 18 is, for Calvin, the definitive statement of progressive sanctification: believers are not instantly perfected but gradually transformed from one degree of glory to another by the Spirit’s work. The transformation is real but incomplete until the eschaton.

The NET note discusses the identification ‘the Lord is the Spirit’: this is not an ontological equation of the second and third persons of the Trinity but a functional identification in the context of the Exodus narrative. The ‘Lord’ before whom Moses removed his veil (Exod 34:34) is now experienced by believers as the Spirit who removes the veil of the old covenant.

The NET note on ‘contemplating’ (katoptrizomenoi) discusses the debated meaning: ‘beholding as in a mirror’ (middle voice) or ‘reflecting’ (passive voice). The NET favours ‘contemplating’—believers gaze at the Lord’s glory and are transformed by what they see.

Fee provides a sustained argument that the ‘Lord’ of v. 17 is Christ identified with the Spirit in experiential terms: the risen Christ is encountered by believers through the Spirit. Fee reads v. 18 as the most comprehensive statement of the Spirit’s transformative work in Paul’s letters: the Spirit progressively conforms believers to the image of Christ through the ‘beholding’ of divine glory.

Harris provides the most meticulous exegesis of vv. 12–18, addressing the multiple interpretive cruxes: (1) the purpose of Moses’ veil (concealment of fading glory, not protection from brightness); (2) the referent of ‘the Lord’ in v. 16 (Christ, identified with the Yahweh of Exod 34:34); (3) the meaning of ‘the Lord is the Spirit’ (functional, not ontological identification); (4) the sense of katoptrizomenoi in v. 18 (beholding, not reflecting). Harris argues that v. 18 is the theological summit of chs. 3–4: the new-covenant community is defined by unveiled, transformative encounter with divine glory through the Spirit.

rows.label: vv.1–3
rows.text: Letters of recommendation: the Corinthians as a living epistle from Christ

rows.label: vv.4–6
rows.text: New-covenant ministry: competence from God, letter vs. Spirit

rows.label: vv.7–11
rows.text: Surpassing glory: three a fortiori comparisons between old and new covenants

rows.label: vv.12–16
rows.text: Moses’ veil: concealment of fading glory, removal in Christ

rows.label: vv.17–18
rows.text: The Lord is the Spirit: unveiled contemplation and progressive transformation

lit.note: Chapter 3 is Paul’s most sustained new-covenant theology, comparing the glory of the Sinai covenant with the surpassing glory of the Spirit’s ministry. The veil typology and transformation language are among the most influential passages in Pauline theology.

themes.note: Covenant dominates as Paul develops the most explicit new-covenant theology in the Pauline corpus. Holiness scores highly through the transformation imagery of v. 18.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: Their transformed lives are a ‘letter from Christ’ (v…

ppl.name: Moses
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Glory of the New Covenant

translations.text: Now if the ministry that brought death, which was engraved in letters on stone, came with glory, so that the Israelites could not look steadily at the face of Moses because of its glory, transitory though it was,
Now if the ministry of death, carved in letters on stone, came with such glory that the Israelites could not gaze at Moses’ face because of its glory, which was being brought to an end,
You yourselves are our letter, written on our hearts, known and read by everyone.
You yourselves are our letter of recommendation, written on our hearts, to be known and read by all.

Reads The Glory of the New Covenant within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
